notes.anchor: decrees of the Senate, edicts, stories, rumours
notes.note: The contents of the running news-digest (the acta urbana) Caelius hired a man to compile: senatorial decrees, magistrates' edicts, and the daily gossip of the city. This kind of paid clipping-service kept absent governors abreast of Rome.

notes.anchor: the assemblies of the men beyond the Po
notes.note: The Transpadane Gauls north of the river Po held Latin rights but not full Roman citizenship; rumours that Caesar might enfranchise them through a comitia were a live and inflammatory issue in 51 BC.

notes.anchor: the succession to the Gallic provinces
notes.note: The question of when and how Caesar's command in Gaul would be handed to a successor; the central constitutional struggle of 51-50 BC that led to civil war. Marcellus's stalling here is the opening move.
The first day of June in the Roman calendar (the Kalends being the first of any month). Senate business was routinely postponed to a named future date.

notes.anchor: the loafers in front of the rostra
notes.note: The subrostrani, idlers who hung about the speaker's platform (the Rostra) in the Forum and traded in rumour; Caelius treats them as an unreliable Roman gossip-mill.
Caelius's deflating joke: the supposed roadside murderer Q. Pompeius Rufus was in fact reduced to keeping public baths at the seaside resort of Baulis, too poor and starved to kill anyone.
